---
title: "Assessing Treatment Risks in Parkinson Disease Psychosis"
---

## Overview

Parkinson disease psychosis (PDP) is one of the most challenging non-motor complications of Parkinson disease, often emerging as disease progresses and cognitive impairment worsens. It is associated with increased caregiver burden, institutionalization, and mortality, yet treatment options remain limited and comparative safety across antipsychotic agents remains uncertain.

This project addresses a clinically motivated question: whether real-world differences in antipsychotic prescribing patterns in PDP are associated with meaningful differences in mortality and downstream health outcomes. In particular, uncertainty remains regarding the safety profile of pimavanserin compared to commonly used off-label agents such as quetiapine.

My role in this study focused on constructing a clinically valid cohort from electronic health record data, defining exposure and outcome structures under real-world constraints, and developing the statistical framework to evaluate treatment-associated mortality risk.

---

## Data & Study Design

We conducted a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from the UC San Diego Health System between 2016 and 2019. The initial Parkinson disease cohort included approximately 3,000 patients, from which individuals with psychosis were identified using diagnostic codes and antipsychotic medication records.

A critical component of the study involved manual chart review for patients prescribed antipsychotics to confirm that medications were being used for psychosis rather than alternative indications such as sleep disturbance or mood disorders. This step improved cohort validity and reduced exposure misclassification.

After applying exclusion criteria (including primary psychiatric disorders and atypical parkinsonism), the final analytic cohort included 315 patients categorized into four treatment groups: untreated, pimavanserin, quetiapine, and combination therapy.

A key methodological limitation was that medication exposure in the EHR lacked precise start and stop timing, requiring exposure to be operationalized as a time-fixed indicator across the study period.

---

## Statistical Approach

Given limitations in temporal resolution, we used a cross-sectional outcome framework rather than survival-based modeling.

Primary analyses included descriptive comparisons across treatment groups followed by logistic regression models for mortality. Models were sequentially adjusted for key confounders:

- Age  
- Sex  
- Levodopa equivalent daily dose (LEDD)  
- Dementia diagnosis  

Secondary analyses examined hospitalization patterns, motor severity, cognitive status, and medication dose distributions across treatment groups.

Due to incomplete exposure timing, Cox proportional hazards models were not feasible, and logistic regression was selected as the primary analytic approach.

---

## Key Findings

Pimavanserin use was associated with lower odds of mortality compared to untreated patients after adjustment for key clinical confounders. In contrast, quetiapine and combination therapy did not show statistically significant differences in mortality relative to the untreated group.

Across groups, treated patients generally had higher dopaminergic medication exposure and more severe motor symptoms, while untreated patients demonstrated a higher burden of selected comorbidities. However, these differences did not fully explain the observed mortality pattern.

No clear dose–response relationship was observed within quetiapine users.

---

## Analytical Challenges & Decisions

This study required balancing clinical realism with methodological constraints inherent to electronic health record data. Key challenges included:

- Exposure definition prioritizing chart-confirmed clinical use over precise temporal measurement  
- Mortality modeled as a binary outcome due to incomplete time-to-event structure  
- Residual confounding from unmeasured disease severity and prescribing rationale  
- Selection bias reflecting real-world treatment decisions rather than randomization  

The analysis was therefore framed as a real-world comparative effectiveness study rather than a causal inference design.

---

## Impact

This project contributed to clinical understanding of antipsychotic safety in Parkinson disease psychosis, supporting the possibility that pimavanserin may be associated with a more favorable mortality profile compared to untreated patients.

Methodologically, it strengthened experience in:

- Cohort construction from EHR data  
- Chart review–validated exposure definition  
- Handling confounding in observational pharmacoepidemiology  
- Interpreting treatment effects under real-world constraints  

Overall, the project reinforced the importance of aligning statistical methods with data limitations rather than imposing idealized assumptions on complex clinical datasets.
